# Overview {.unnumbered}

<!--
WRITE THIS PARAGRAPH FIRST — on day one, before any experiment. It is the
"abstract you wish you could publish." State the question, why it matters, and
the one-sentence claim you hope to make. You will revise it every loop; that
revision is how you steer the research. Follow Drela's summary template:
purpose → mechanism → key novel property → what this book contains.
-->

**The claim, in one sentence.** _State here the single takeaway a reader should
leave with._ This book builds that claim from first principles and tests it
against evidence, so that by the end you don't just believe it — you see *why*
it has to be true, and exactly where it stops being true.

_One paragraph of motivation:_ what real problem does this solve, and why should
the reader care? Frame it as requirement → consequence (Drela): "doing X well
requires Y; Y enables Z; therefore this work matters because…". Lead with a
concrete magnitude if you have one.

## The headline

Lead with the money result — the single figure or number the whole book earns —
and immediately guard it against the obvious misreading:

::: {.callout-note title="How to read this figure"}
State plainly what this result does **not** say (e.g. "this is not a like-for-like
architecture ranking, and it does not claim attention is worse in general"), and
what was held constant to make the comparison fair. Pre-empting the misread is
part of showing the data honestly.
:::

If the project is falsification-driven, a compact ledger of what survived and what
didn't orients the reader fast:

| Hypothesis | Verdict | Where |
|---|---|---|
| _the central claim_ | Confirmed | @sec-chapter |
| _a tempting alternative_ | **Refuted** | @sec-nb-iter11 |

## The whole idea in one picture

Give the reader the mental model *before* the details, so every later piece has
somewhere to attach. A flowchart, a schematic, or a single hero figure.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    A[Problem / input] --> B[Mechanism]
    B --> C[Prediction]
    C --> D[Test against evidence]
    D -->|refines| B
```

## What's different here

A comparison table orients the reader fast — what does this approach do that the
obvious baseline doesn't? (Direct, honest, one axis per row.)

| Aspect | Baseline | This work |
|---|---|---|
| _dimension 1_ | … | … |
| _dimension 2_ | … | … |
| _dimension 3_ | … | … |

## Reading order

Hand the reader the path explicitly — a research book is a guided journey, not a
data dump. Sequence: motivation → build the mechanism from first principles →
evidence → honesty (what's *not* solved) → context.

1. **[Chapter title](chapter.qmd)** — what it establishes and what it hands the next chapter.
2. _…_

## Scope — what this book does and does not do

State the boundaries up front (Drela): the specific regime this addresses, and
what is deliberately out of scope (and where that omitted piece is handled
instead). Candor here builds trust for everything after.
